title: Rebuilding Trust After You've Finally Spoken Up
dek: The words are out of your mouth, and now your body is shaking. Here is how to tend to the somatic aftermath of telling the truth, and how to rebuild trust with the animal of your own body.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Body After the Words Spill Out


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.171Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You’ve just spoken up - really spoken, the kind of words that taste foreign on your tongue, words you’ve rehearsed in the quiet corners of your mind but never dared to send out into the air. The moment the syllables leave your mouth, your body doesn’t simply relax. No, it’s a raw, electric thing - like a live wire buzzing beneath your skin. The vulnerability hangover crashes in fast, and it’s not metaphorical; it’s every muscle waking up in a chorus of tension and tremble.

Your masseter tightens first, the jaw muscle clenching so hard it feels like it might snap. You can trace the strain just beneath your earlobe, the stubborn knot of fascia refusing to release. This is the muscle that guards your words, clenched tight through years of silence, now protesting the sudden breach. Your shoulders - those habitual armor plates - rise involuntarily. The trapezius and levator scapulae coil like springs, pulling your neck into a rigid frame. You can almost smell the sharp, metallic tang of adrenaline sweating out of your pores, slick on the skin at the nape of your neck and the small of your back.

Your diaphragm, usually the gentle bellows of breath, feels erratic, fluttering like a trapped bird. The breath is shallow, quick, the ribs barely expanding before collapsing inward again. Your psoas muscles, central players in your core stability, knot up in a protective clench. They coil deep within the belly, a visceral tightening that roots you to the floor but also keeps you on edge, ready for fight or flight even after the danger has passed.

Your hands tremble, not with weakness but with the rawness of exposure. The nervous system spins on a loop of “Did I say enough? Too much? Was I heard?” Your throat feels dry, a fine film of salt on your tongue as the body’s chemistry shifts beneath your skin. This is not just emotional fallout; this is the biology of vulnerability, a cascade of neurochemicals sparking your muscles and fascia into a state of alert.

But here’s the thing: speaking up isn’t an endpoint. It’s a beginning - a seismic shift in the architecture of trust you’re rebuilding with yourself. The body remembers every moment it was silenced, every time the masseter clenched in restraint, every time the psoas tightened against the fear of being seen. Now, when those muscles flare, it’s a signal, an invitation to witness the tremors and the sweat as proof of movement. You’re navigating the delicate terrain between old survival patterns and new possibilities for presence.

The vulnerability hangover is fierce because it’s honest. It carries the weight of history but also the promise of reknitting the threads between your breath, your voice, and your body’s wisdom. The shaking hands, the taut muscles, the fluttering diaphragm - they are not failures. They are the first tremors of a new alignment, a somatic map back to your own unfettered voice.

## The Body Holds Its Breath in the Waiting

You’re sitting there, phone in hand or maybe across from them at a kitchen table, and the seconds stretch out like cold molasses. Your diaphragm tightens first, a subtle clench beneath your ribs that feels like the breath you’re supposed to take but can’t quite catch. It isn’t just nervousness; it’s the body’s way of bracing. The diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle separating your chest from your belly, clamps down, trapping the air and holding your breath hostage. It’s the body’s earliest alarm bell, the same muscle that stiffens in moments of threat, when your system prepares to defend or flee even before your mind processes the danger.

Then, almost simultaneously, the muscles at the back of your neck flare into action. The levator scapulae, those small but fierce muscles running from the top of your shoulder blades up to the sides of your neck, tighten and contract. They pull your shoulders up and inward, creating that familiar, aching tension that makes your neck feel like it’s caught in a slow, unrelenting vise. It’s the body’s way of preparing for impact - physical or emotional - readying you to shield your head or hold it high with defiance. You might notice the exact spot where the muscle fibers knot, just beneath the base of your skull, radiating a dull throb or sharp zing that twists down into your trapezius and beyond.

This is not just about the moment you’re in. The body remembers, always. It catalogs every betrayal, every slight, every moment you felt your boundaries were crossed or your fury dismissed. The diaphragm and levator scapulae don’t just tighten because you’re waiting for a reaction now - they respond because they know this waiting. They’ve been here before, in the quiet heartbreak of being ignored, in the sharp sting of being disbelieved, in the cold wound of being dismissed. This is biology, not pathology. It is your nervous system’s archive of survival, a deep muscle memory encoded not in your headspace but in your fascia, your tendons, your very cells.

Teachers like Peter Levine and Bessel van der Kolk remind us that trauma isn’t a glitch in the system - it’s the system doing exactly what it’s supposed to do: protect you. The diaphragm’s clench, the levator scapulae’s brace - they are your body’s way of saying, “I’ve been here before. I’m ready.” This is not weakness or dysfunction. This is fierce, embodied wisdom. The body’s not turning against you; it’s rallying, holding the line in the invisible warfields of your relationships.

So when you sit there, breath caught in your chest and neck stiff as winter branches, know this: your body is speaking in its oldest language. It is not pathology. It is biology. It is you, in your full, unyielding human truth.

## Anger as Boundary’s Fierce Midwife

Late at night, when the house exhales its last breath of quiet and the weight of silence settles heavy on your trapezius and levator scapulae, that’s when you feel it: the stirrings of anger. It’s not the wild beast you were told to tame or the ugly crack in your otherwise smooth self. It is, as Karla McLaren teaches, the body's raw, electric insistence that your boundaries have been breached and need urgent repair.

McLaren’s work insists we stop treating anger as a moral failing or a flaw of character. Instead, she reframes it as a boundary-restoring messenger, a vital force that surges through your masseter muscle and down into your diaphragm, giving you the energy to protect yourself. The body knows this well. That tight clench in your jaw, the quickening pulse in your temple - it’s biology, not pathology. Anger is the energy you need to say “no” loudly and clearly when your limits are crossed.

This isn’t about the moment the words fly out, though. The way your voice cracks or how your hands tremble in that flash of fury is just the tip of the iceberg. What McLaren insists on, and what your body demands, is that you honor that energy long after the confrontation ends. That simmering heat beneath your ribs, the low hum in your psoas as it braces and coils, is the body’s way of insisting you don’t let the boundary slip back open once the storm passes.

You see, anger doesn’t dissolve once you've spoken your truth. It lingers in the fascia and the sinews, a reminder that your personal space, your emotional territory, was invaded. To ignore that energy is to invite the boundary’s collapse - and to disrespect the body’s fierce warning system. McLaren gives us a map: listen to that energy, track its course through your muscles, and use it to rebuild your defenses with intention, not shame.

This is why, when you feel the familiar burn in your cheeks or the pressure behind your eyes, it’s worth sitting with it, not wilting away. Your anger is not a sign that you’ve failed, but that your inner land is still sacred. The energy coursing through you right now is the very force that will stitch back the edges of your selfhood, tight and resilient.

In this way, anger is not just a momentary flare but a sustained current of protection. It feeds you the power to hold your boundaries firm, long after the words have faded into the night. The body remembers. The jaw remembers. And so must you.

## The Body Remembers: Resmaa Menakem and Somatic Settling

You know that tight coil in your neck - the levator scapulae knot that flares up when you’ve said the words you’ve been holding back. That’s not just tension; it’s tissue memory. Resmaa Menakem’s work insists on this truth: trauma, anger, and the stories we carry aren’t confined to the brain or the mind. They live deep in the body’s architecture, in the fascia that wraps around your muscles and bones, in the psoas that tightens beneath your abdomen when danger whispers in your ear. His teaching on somatic settling is a radical invitation: don’t rush past the body’s tremors and quivers after you’ve spoken your truth. Let the tissues rest. Let them remember safety.

When you finally uncoil that clenched jaw - the masseter muscle that’s been holding your fury like a secret - the body isn’t instantly at peace. It needs time to integrate the act of speaking out, of standing firm, of not swallowing your anger. Menakem names this process “somatic settling,” and it’s as vital as the breath moving in and out of your diaphragm at 3 a.m., when the house is quiet except for your own shifting weight. The tissues, once activated by the energy of your anger, can’t just snap back to neutral like a rubber band. They ripple with echoes, muscle fibers twitching, fascia thickening or loosening, nervous system on a slow descent from fight to rest.

He teaches that trauma’s imprint is cellular, held in the body long after the triggering event has passed. That’s why your trapezius tightens when someone raises a voice or when the sky turns slate gray with storm clouds. Your tissues remember before your mind does. This is not pathology; this is biology. When you speak your truth, you’re not only engaging your voice but also stirring the sediment that’s settled in your tissues - past injuries, ancestral pain, every time you were told to be quiet.

Menakem’s guidance is precise: after the surge of speaking out, find the moments to listen to your body’s language. Maybe it’s a slow rocking in a chair, the feeling of your feet firmly on the cold floor, or the subtle release in your belly as the psoas unwinds its grip. These aren’t just calming rituals; they’re the body’s way of saying, “I’m safe now.” You’re teaching your nervous system that this anger is not a threat but a message. The tissues begin to soften, the fascia loosens its grasp, and the body settles back into its natural state of ease.

In practice, this means you don’t rush to “get over” your anger or force yourself into calm. You honor the trembling shoulders, the tight throat, the restless fingers. You trust that your body knows how to move through this, if you give it the time and space. Menakem’s work reminds you that healing is not just psychological - it’s somatic, held in the very fibers of your being. The body remembers. And when you listen, truly listen, it will teach you how to settle.

## Herbal Support for the Nervous System During This Rebuilding Phase

When your nervous system is frayed, it’s not just a metaphor. The levator scapulae knots up in the back of your neck by late afternoon, the diaphragm clamps down tight as if bracing against some invisible blow, and the masseter clenches so hard you wake with a jaw that aches like you’ve been grinding stones. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your body remembers the tension, the vigilance, the long nights when sleep slipped away like smoke through cracked windows. To rebuild from this, you need more than rest and good intentions - you need herbs that know the body intimately, that work in the fascia and muscle, in the subtle electrical hum of the nervous system itself.

Milky Oats (Avena sativa) stands out as a cornerstone in this phase. It’s not a trivial oat you find in a bowl at breakfast but the green oat tops, harvested just as the grain begins to form. This herb is a true nervine tonic, rich in minerals and alkaloids that nourish the nervous tissue and build resilience. It’s especially well-known for supporting the psoas muscle, that deep core stabilizer that holds so much of your emotional and physical tension. When you take Milky Oats as a tincture or simmer it into a strong tea, you’re feeding your system something it’s been starving for - the quiet strength to hold space for your anger without it fracturing your nerves.

Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) is another herb you want close at hand. The nervine action here is calming but not sedating; it’s as if Skullcap whispers to the trapezius and the masseter, coaxing them to release their grip, to stop holding the world’s weight on your shoulders and jaw. Traditional Chinese Medicine has long valued Skullcap for its ability to clear heat and calm the liver, which in TCM governs the flow of qi and, by extension, emotional balance. You’ll smell a faint, green bitterness in the tincture, a reminder that real medicine often carries complexity, not sugar-coating.

Wood Betony (Stachys officinalis), sometimes called “the herb of the mind,” rounds out this trio with its grounding, centering qualities. It works gently on the nervous system, easing the levator scapulae and the fascia around your cervical spine. Wood Betony has a long folk history of easing anxiety and nervous tension without dulling your edge. It’s medicine for the parts of you that remember being told your fury was ugly, unwelcome, and so you stuffed it down until it tightened your throat and clenched your fists in sleep.

For practical application, a product like [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20) combines these herbs thoughtfully, delivering a potent blend that respects the complexity of the nervous system’s needs. When you take this tonic, you are not medicating a problem but supporting a process - the slow, patient unwinding of trauma held in muscle and fascia, the rebuilding of your capacity to feel anger without it ripping you apart inside.

This is serious medicine for serious work. It’s not a quick fix but a steady companion through the nights when your body remembers what it’s been through, and you’re learning how to be fiercely alive in your own skin again.

## An Embodied Practice for Grounding the Body After Speaking Up

You’ve just spoken your truth - maybe a little fierce, maybe trembling in places you didn’t expect. Your chest still feels taut, like the diaphragm’s been braced against a storm, ribs expanding unevenly as if uncertain whether to keep breathing or hold tight. Now, it’s time to come home to yourself, to let the body settle from that surge of energy, that flood of adrenaline and heat that anger stirs in the fascia and muscle.

Find a place where your feet can root firmly - bare floor if you can, so you can really feel the texture of the earth beneath you. Stand or sit, whichever feels more accessible right now. Begin by noticing the weight of your body. The trapezius and levator scapulae muscles in your neck might still be tight, pulling your shoulders up toward your ears. Consciously let them drop. Imagine the tension draining down your spine, each vertebra in your spine loosening, the cervical curve gently softening as the sternocleidomastoid and masseter release their grip. This is not a push to relax, but a soft yielding to the body’s natural pull downward.

Now, bring your attention to your breath - the breath that was likely shallow and quick while you spoke your mind. Place one hand lightly over your solar plexus, just above the stomach, and the other on your lower belly. Inhale slowly through your nose, feeling the diaphragm descend, pushing your lower hand outward as your belly expands. The breath should be quiet but full, as if you’re drawing in the cool, damp air of an early morning forest before the sun rises. Exhale gently through parted lips, allowing your belly to soften and your ribs to fall.

Repeat this breathing cycle five to seven times, each inhale anchoring you more deeply into your body, each exhale a release of residual tension. As you breathe, notice the subtle shifts: your feet pressing more firmly into the ground, the small unfolding of the chest, the loosening of the jaw where clenched teeth might have resided. This isn’t about forcing calm, but about handing your body a moment to remember its own rhythm, to let the anger’s message settle into the muscles rather than rattle in the mind.

If your hands feel restless, try gently rubbing the fleshy pads of your thumbs or rolling your shoulders slowly forward and back, paying attention to the levator scapulae where anger often lodges. You might catch a faint scent of your own skin - warm, a little salty from exertion. This is your body speaking back to you, grounding you in the now, steady and alive.

After this practice, you will find yourself less caught in the surge and more held by your own physical presence. The body remembers what the mind sometimes forgets: this is not pathology, this is biology. Your fury is not ugliness - it’s a messenger, and now it has a place to land.

## FAQ: Aftermath of Speaking Up, Fear of Abandonment, and Physical Exhaustion

**Q: Why do I feel so drained and shaky after I finally speak my anger aloud?**  
**A:** That tremor in your hands, the sudden drop in energy, the tightness in your diaphragm - this is your body recalibrating after years of holding your fury in. When you speak your anger, especially if you’ve been taught it’s “ugly” or dangerous, your nervous system fires up like a storm breaking after drought. The trapezius muscles in your neck might knot, your jaw (the masseter) clenches and then relaxes, leaving a strange residual ache. This isn’t weakness; it’s your biology shifting from fight-or-flight back to rest. The body remembers the tension patterns, and as the fascia loosens, the exhaustion floods in. It’s like running a marathon without training - the nervous system and muscles need time to recover. Herbs like chamomile or passionflower steeped hot can soothe the nervous system’s frayed edges, while gentle movement - perhaps a slow walk at dusk when the air smells of damp earth - helps process the embodiment of that anger.

**Q: I’m terrified that if I express my anger, people will leave me. How do I handle that?**  
**A:** The fear of abandonment is a tight knot in the levator scapulae, that muscle running from your neck to your shoulder blade, often pulled taut when you brace for loss. This fear is ancient, wired into the survival circuits that tell you connection equals safety. But here’s the truth your body knows: silence isn’t safety - it’s invisibility. When you speak your anger, you’re not pushing people away; you’re inviting them to meet the real you, the you that the jaw remembers biting down to keep quiet. It’s terrifying because it cracks the old scripts, but it also creates space for authentic connection. Trust that the body’s tremors and the quickened breath are messengers, not failures. The fear is real but transient; your voice is your medicine.

**Q: Sometimes, after expressing anger, I feel a wave of sadness or grief. Is that normal?**  
**A:** Absolutely. Anger and grief are neighbors in the body’s landscape. When you release anger, you unsettle the psoas muscle - the core of your fight response - and in that unmooring, hidden sorrow often surfaces. It’s not a breakdown; it’s a breakthrough. The body is telling you what words alone cannot: there is loss, disappointment, unmet needs beneath the fury. This sadness may come as a lump in the throat or a heaviness in the chest, a signal to slow down and honor the full spectrum of your feeling body. Let yourself cry, hold yourself with gentle herbal tea and soft heat on the ribs. This is not pathology; this is biology, the body clearing space for your next breath.
